title: Using a repair cafe: what they fix and how to prepare
subtitle: Finding a repair cafe near you, what to bring, and what typically gets repaired vs replaced
excerpt: A repair cafe is a free community event where volunteers with practical skills fix household items people bring in. Repair cafes handle electrical appliances, clothing, furniture, bikes, crockery, and electronics. Around 90 are registered with the Repair Cafe Foundation in the UK, many more operate informally. Success rates vary by item type.
sourceNotes: Repair Cafe Foundation UK directory and annual impact report 2023. iFixit repair culture data for common household appliances. WRAP circular economy strategy for repair and reuse. Restart Project statistics on successful repair rates by category. UK government Right to Repair legislation (2021) for electronic goods.

glossaryTerms.definition: An economic model that keeps materials and products in use for as long as possible through reuse, repair, and recycling, extending product lifetimes beyond the linear norm.

content.text: A repair cafe is a free (or donation-based) community event where volunteers with practical skills repair broken items for the public. Most run fortnightly or monthly for 2-4 hours at a community centre, library, or church hall. Volunteers include electricians, seamstresses, woodworkers, jewellers, and electronics engineers. Attendance is walk-in; some events take advance bookings for specific categories. The
principle here is straightforward: a working kettle repaired for free is a kettle that doesn't enter the waste stream.
The Restart Project tracks repair outcomes across repair events:
Clothing and textiles: around 85% fixed. Common repairs are zips, hems, buttons, and seams. Bring matching thread if you have any.

Small electrical appliances (toasters, kettles, food processors): around 60-65% fixed. Common fault: loose connection, failed element, seized motor. Not fixable: items with obsolete or unavailable spare parts.
Electronics (radios, lamps, small gadgets): around 65% fixed. Power supply faults, dry solder joints, and blown fuses are the common repairable failures.
Computers and phones: lower success rate (40-50%). Depends heavily on the model and availability of parts. iPhones with cracked screens: high success rate at events with a phone specialist.
Bikes: around 75% fixed for common faults (punctures, brake cables, derailleurs). Bring the whole bike; not just the wheel.
Find your local repair cafe: repaircafe.org lists registered events. Local Transition Town groups and community centres often run unregistered events too.
Bring the item clean and as complete as possible: a lamp with its bulb and shade, an appliance with its power lead. If you have already attempted repair and have removed parts, bring those too.
If the item has an obvious fault (the switch crackles, the heating element doesn't glow, the zip pull broke off), describe it clearly to the volunteer. Clear symptom descriptions speed the diagnosis.
Bring any spare parts you already have: spare zips for sewing repairs, replacement bulbs, spare fuses. Repair cafes often run low on consumables toward the end of an event.
